name: Medina de Rioseco and Tordehumos Castle Loop
name_original: Medina de Rioseco desde Castromonte – circular por Castillo de Tordehumos
description: This moderate 34.1-mile road cycling route from Medina de Rioseco explores Castilian fields and offers views of Castillo de Tordehumos.
region_intro: Road cycling around La Mudarra features routes across the vast, open plains of Tierra de Campos, characterized by gently rolling agricultural landscapes and historical landmarks. The region offers a generally flat to undulating terrain, making it suitable for various road cycling abilities. Routes often follow quiet country roads, passing through small villages and alongside rivers like the Sequillo. The area's low elevation gains provide accessible road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around La Mudarra The most popular road cycling route is Medina de Rioseco and Tordehumos Castle Loop, a 34.1 miles (54.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 13 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects historical towns and offers views of ancient castles. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Tierra de Campos and Montealegre Castle Loop, a moderate 48.1 miles (77.4 km) path. This route traverses the expansive plains of Tierra de Campos, featuring open vistas and the historic Montealegre Castle. Local road cyclists also love the Villabrágima from Medina de Rioseco – circular by río Sequillo, a 16.9 miles (27.1 km) trail leading through agricultural fields and alongside the río Sequillo, often completed in about 1 hour 7 minutes. Road cycling around La Mudarra is defined by open plains, agricultural landscapes, and historical villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to longer, moderate routes. The routes in La Mudarra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Mudarra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around La Mudarra?
A: The region of La Mudarra is characterized by the vast, open plains of Tierra de Campos. You'll find gently rolling agricultural landscapes and generally flat to undulating terrain, with low elevation gains. Routes often follow quiet country roads, passing through small villages and alongside rivers like the Sequillo.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in La Mudarra?
A: Yes, for an easier ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166185123/villabragima-from-medina-de-rioseco-circular-by-rio-sequillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villabrágima from Medina de Rioseco – circular by río Sequillo&lt;/a&gt;. This 27.1 km route is rated easy and leads through agricultural fields and alongside the río Sequillo, making it a pleasant option for those seeking a less strenuous experience.

Q: What are some longer distance road cycling routes in the area?
A: For longer distances, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166490018/tierra-de-campos-and-montealegre-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Campos and Montealegre Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; covers 77.4 km, offering expansive plains and open vistas. Another moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166704010/medina-de-rioseco-and-tordehumos-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medina de Rioseco and Tordehumos Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 54.9 km and connects historical towns.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in La Mudarra?
A: Many routes in La Mudarra are designed as circular loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166185123/villabragima-from-medina-de-rioseco-circular-by-rio-sequillo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villabrágima from Medina de Rioseco – circular by río Sequillo&lt;/a&gt; is a great circular option. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1243699824/belmonte-de-campos-from-medina-de-rioseco-circular-via-villarramiel" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belmonte de Campos from Medina de Rioseco — circular via Villarramiel&lt;/a&gt;, offering a 52.3 km loop.

Q: What historical landmarks or points of interest can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Road cycling routes in La Mudarra often pass by historical sites. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166704010/medina-de-rioseco-and-tordehumos-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medina de Rioseco and Tordehumos Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers views of ancient castles, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166490018/tierra-de-campos-and-montealegre-castle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Campos and Montealegre Castle Loop&lt;/a&gt; features the historic Montealegre Castle. You can also explore highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/890605" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla Dock&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5815038" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santiago Apóstol in Medina de Rioseco&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in La Mudarra?
A: Given the open plains and agricultural landscapes, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in La Mudarra. Temperatures are milder, and the scenery is often at its best. Summers can be hot and exposed, while winters can be chilly.

Q: Are there any routes with significant elevation gain for advanced cyclists?
A: While La Mudarra is known for its generally low elevation gains, some routes offer more challenging climbs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33665850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Castroverde de Cerrato – Church of Santa María de Wamba loop from Peñaflor de Hornija&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, includes an elevation gain of over 400 meters, providing a more demanding experience for advanced riders.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in La Mudarra?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in La Mudarra, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the quiet country roads, the expansive open vistas of the Tierra de Campos, and the opportunity to explore historical villages and landmarks without significant climbs.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in La Mudarra pass through small villages and towns like Medina de Rioseco. These settlements typically offer local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments, a meal, or to refill water bottles.

Q: What should I wear for road cycling in La Mudarra?
A: Due to the open nature of the plains, weather conditions can change. It's advisable to wear layers, even on sunny days, to adapt to temperature fluctuations. A windproof jacket is often useful, especially during cooler months or if you encounter headwinds across the open landscapes. Always bring sufficient water, as shade can be limited.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in La Mudarra?
A: There are several road cycling routes around La Mudarra, offering various distances and difficulty levels. The komoot community has explored a good number of these, with options ranging from easy loops to longer, moderate rides across the region's unique landscape.
tips: Built on the ruins of a Roman fort, hence the name Castroalmonde, the most notable monument is near the Plaza de la Constitución and admires the 16th-century Church of the Immaculate Conception.

Villabrágima is a small town in the Tierra de Campos region with origins in Roman times. Take a look at the Iglesia Parroquial de Santa María and cycle through the Puerta del Reloj de Villabrágima. A great photo opportunity.

View of the 12th century castle that dominates the landscape and the trade route between the ancient kingdoms of Castilla y León. The defensive castle of Tordehumos, located on top of a hill, is full of history.

Medina de Rioseco is known as the seat of the Admiralty of Castilla, the city has a rich history that goes back to the Vacceos, Celts, Romans. Located next to the sequillo river, I recommend you tour the town by bike and visit the Church of Santiago Apóstol.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a moderate road cycling route. It requires a good level of fitness, but primarily features paved surfaces. Be prepared for rolling hills typical of the Castilian plateau.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike tour?
faqs.answer: The estimated moving time for this racebike tour is approximately 2 hours and 12 minutes. However, your total trip duration may vary depending on stops for sightseeing and rest.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Medina de Rioseco from Castromonte circular route?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the Tierra de Campos region, known for its expansive Castilian fields and agricultural landscapes. You'll primarily ride on quiet, well-paved roads through rolling hills, offering wide-open views. While mostly paved, some challenging segments might be encountered.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Tierra de Campos region is particularly picturesque in autumn when the channel areas take on remarkable ochre tones. Spring and early summer would also offer pleasant cycling conditions before the peak heat of summer.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers several points of interest. You can see the Church of the Immaculate Conception of Castromonte, the Puerta del Reloj of Villabrágima, and enjoy a View of Tordehumos Castle. In Medina de Rioseco, you can visit the Church of Santiago Apóstol and explore the city's historical architecture.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting from Castromonte and passing through Medina de Rioseco, you can typically find parking in either town. Medina de Rioseco, being a larger town, likely offers more designated parking options. Look for public parking areas near the town centers.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its 'moderate' difficulty and length of nearly 55 km, this route is generally more suitable for cyclists with a good level of fitness and experience. It might be challenging for younger children or beginner cyclists.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route primarily on public roads. While there aren't specific restrictions against dogs, it's generally not advisable to bring dogs on a racebike route of this distance and type for their safety and yours. If you do, they would need to be securely transported.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, this is a public road cycling route through the Valladolid province, and you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle it.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Madrid Way of Saint James, the Colada de Valdnebro de los Valles al Batán, and the Cañada Real Leonesa Occidental.

faqs.question: What kind of cultural and historical experiences can I expect?
faqs.answer: The route combines natural beauty with rich historical elements. Medina de Rioseco, known as the 'City of Admirals,' offers impressive churches and ancient city gates. The presence of the Castillo de Tordehumos adds a historical landmark, and you'll experience the serene rural life and cultural depth of the Valladolid province.

waypoints.name: Church of the Immaculate Conception of Castromonte
Church of Santiago Apóstol, Medina de Rioseco
